paragraphs.body: CHAPTER 2: What a Coincidence

The following morning, I thought it was weird waking up to no Madeline. Her bed is empty just like it was last night before I fell asleep, but I decide not to think too much about it. She mentioned yesterday that she was a morning person, so that’s probably it. It’s the first day of class after all. Though, my mind is preoccupied at the moment with thoughts of what happened in the shower to be thinking about her early class schedule. I remember the silhouette of the figure who was watching me and their black shoes standing inches away from me. I remember the deafening sound of water hitting the floor and the wrinkling of my skin underneath the water from being in there so long. Even now, my heartbeat picks up to an elevated rhythm as I think about my back pressed up against the cool tiled walls, clutching myself in a desperate attempt to cover my nakedness in case the voyeur were to make a move. If only I had the damn courage to pull back the curtain to see whoever it was, maybe then I wouldn’t have to stare over my shoulder as I brush my teeth in the communal bathroom. I spit the toothpaste into the porcelain sink before wiping any residue from my mouth. Taking a deep breath, I shut my eyes and cup some running water into my hands. I lean closer to the sink, splashing my face with the warm water in an attempt to both wake me up and calm me down.

annotations.comment: Verb tense shifting around.
annotations.context_left: The following morning, I thought it was weird waking up to no Madeline. Her bed
annotations.context_right: empty just like it was last night before I fell asleep, but I decide not to think too much about it.

annotations.text: remember the silhouette of the figure who was watching me and their black shoes standing inches away from me. I remember the deafening sound of water hitting the floor and the wrinkling of my skin underneath the water from being in there so long. Even now, my heartbeat picks up to an elevated rhythm as I think about my back pressed up against the cool tiled walls, clutching myself in a desperate attempt to cover my nakedness in case the voyeur were to make a move
annotations.comment: This is effective writing but remember that we would’ve just read this scene — don’t need to overly reintroduce it.

paragraphs.body: SLAM.

My eyes shoot open, and my head whips around to observe the source of the noise. My heart races as I look over to the stalls lined up against the back wall, and a pair of red converse peek out from underneath the stall. I sigh with relief, running my hands through my hair before making my way back to my room to get ready for class. I’ve never been so happy to see the color red.

annotations.comment: Effective, but you might play with having her try to not be scared and then have the door scare her?

paragraphs.body: This lecture hall is bigger than what I’m used to. Community college doesn’t exactly hold spaces for three hundred students at a time. Hell, don’t quote me on this, but I don’t even think I had three hundred people that attended my community college. Other students file in beside me and walk down the sloped steps to begin filing into the many rows of seats presented in front of me. I don’t care to move any further, and I take my seat at the back of the class right next to the door. Echoed conversations sound all around me; some girls talking about a sorority they’re planning on rushing while others ask the person next to them for the wifi password. I scoff and pull out my own laptop, setting it on the tray attached to my chair’s armrest. I take out my earbuds, slipping one into my ear and let the rest of the cord dangle into my lap. I plan on disassociating for the next fifty minutes. That is, until a hand grips my shoulder.

annotations.text: I had three hundred people that attended my community college
annotations.comment: Awkward wording
What is this magical college where students want to sit down front?
Why scoffing?
I lost track a little bit but I’m surprised she’s not even vaguely hopeful the class will be worth her time.

paragraphs.body: I look up to see Madeline’s freckled face smiling widely at me. “Hey, I didn’t know you were taking developmental psych,” I say, chuckling softly.
“I could say the same about you. Crazy coincidence huh?” she says and laughs along with me. “Is this seat taken?”
“Nope. Go for it,” I reply and nod to the seat next to me.

She utters a quick ‘thanks’ before squeezing behind me to get to her seat. She plops down and unzips her backpack before pulling out a pink notebook riddled with stickers. One that catches my eye says “good vibes” which makes me snicker silently to myself. She continues pulling things out of her bag; a water bottle, pencil case, folder, and a jean jacket. “Jeesh, that bag is like a clown car,” I say to her. “The only thing I don’t see in there is a computer.”

annotations.comment: Why?

paragraphs.body: “Yeah, I prefer to write by hand. I remember things better that way,” she tells me before rifling through her pencil case and pulling out a blue ink pen.
“Pretty old-fashion if you ask me. Very old lady of you, Madeline,” I joke.
“I guess so,” she mutters, tucking a loose strand of auburn hair behind her ear, her face heating up.

annotations.comment: Are they having trouble getting back the chummy relationship they had at the diner?

paragraphs.body: I’m about to continue talking to her when a middle-aged woman with gray hair taps on a microphone. The harsh auditory feedback makes many of the students groan and cover their ears. “Sorry about that, class, but we’re going to get started. My name is Professor Richland. Welcome, all of you, to developmental psychology.”
I take her introduction as my cue to pull up a game of solitaire on my laptop and begin to drown out the world around me.

annotations.comment: Again, truly amazing that she just assumes the class sucks.
annotations.context_left: I take her introduction as my cue to pull up a ga
annotations.context_right: of solitaire on my laptop and begin to drown out the world around me.

paragraphs.body: Click.
My mouse hovers over the facedown deck of digital cards, clicking on one to reveal an Ace of Spades-
Click. Click.
I frown, adjusting the earbud in my ears as I try to concentrate on the soothing voice of Frank Ocean as I attempt to shut out whatever the hell is making that damn-
Click. Click. Click Click Click Click Click Click Click Click…

I turn my head slowly to face the culprit who I am about to murder to see none other than Madeline clicking her pen intensely. Her eyes are laser focused on the professor at the front of the room, making it clear that she has no idea how annoying she is right now. “Hey, Mads,” I say in a hushed whisper to get her attention. Her attention is ripped away from the front of the classroom and she looks at me. “Would you mind, uh, chilling out with the pen-clicking? I can’t focus on…the lecture,” I tell her, lying straight through my teeth.

annotations.comment: I love this moment, because it’s complicated. It makes sense for her to ask Mads this, but also — Mads is actually trying to learn? Fascinating.

paragraphs.body: “Oh yeah, I’m sorry. Sometimes I get anxious and I just start fidgeting but I’ll stop, don’t worry,” she whispers back before turning back to face the front. I breathe out a sigh of relief before looking back at my computer to finish out the rest of my game.

The rest of the class went by rather quickly. From what I could gather, the professor just went over the syllabus and what our big project would be for the semester. She dismissed us and said that she’d see us on Wednesday. As if on cue, every student got up and rushed towards the exit. I grab my backpack, slinging it over my shoulder. “Hey, I’ll see you back at the dorm,” I tell Madeline. She smiles and waves goodbye to me before we both head out the double doors and go our separate ways.

annotations.comment: TRY LISTENING.

paragraphs.body: I had an hour to kill before my next class, so I spent that time grabbing a quick bite to eat at the dining hall. Afterwards, I head into my next class of the day. Another big lecture, chemistry, and take a seat at the back of the class once again, leaving a space in between me and the girl to my left. Her hair is jet black, pin straight, and she has a noticeable septum piercing. I catch a glimpse at her phone, seeing her swiping on Tinder. Guy. Guy. Ugly guy. Really ugly guy- girl. Oh she’s into girls too. I turn to her and clear my throat, leaning back in my chair.

annotations.comment: Made me laugh. Maddie is definitely creeping.

paragraphs.body: “Hey, I’m Jesse,” I say coolly. Her lipstick-lined lips quirk up into a smirk.
“Hey, Im-”
“Long time no see!” I hear from behind me. I turn to see Madeline standing in front of the lecture hall doors.
“Hey, are you following me?” I question her in a teasing manner.

She just laughs and takes a seat in between the girl who’s name I didn’t get and me, and she begins pulling out the rest of her things. I look past her slightly to the girl who I was talking to before, and I see that she went back to swiping on her phone. I shrug it off and take out my laptop once again, pulling up the syllabus on one tab before opening another game of solitaire in another. I look over towards Madeline every now and then. She’s once again honed in on the professor, clicking her pen every now and then before apologizing to me in a whisper. I excuse her behavior as the typical freshman first day of class jitters and go back to pretending to take down notes. The rest of the day goes by swiftly. Madeline and I meet up at the dining hall for dinner together, and then we head back to the dorm. A rather uneventful kickoff to the fall semester. She keeps good on her promise and comes to talk to the RA with me about the shower incident. Our RA, Reyna, looks rather puzzled by the whole situation, but gave me some bullshit about how “Rockwell is one of the safest universities in the country” and she’s sure it’s a “harmless prank.” Madeline gave me a reassuring pat on the back before we head back to our dorm for the night. I feel filthy, but something tells me not to shower tonight.

annotations.comment: Are they both freshmen? (Again, sorry, I can’t recall)You do a lot of summarizing here, but I think you’re including more than you need to. You cold combine the girl on Tinder who the narrator doesn’t get to meet with Maddie’s arrival as in the first class and then just mention that she was in the next class too, instead of giving us two separate incidents.
I wondered if the narrator wanted to do this or not.

paragraphs.body: I didn’t think much of Madeline being in two of my classes. It’s relatively normal considering they’re big lecture halls. But then it became three; and then four. It's the same story: me walking into the room, sitting at the back of the class, trying to make a connection with someone else in the room and then getting “cock-blocked” by Madeline. It is now Tuesday afternoon, and I have one last class in my schedule that I have not been to yet. I just got off the waitlist for it, but, on the off chance that Madeline is in my twenty-person sociology class, I decide to sit towards the front where she wouldn’t expect me to be. I try to reassure myself, but for the last few days I’ve lived, eaten, and breathed nothing but Madeline. If I’m hungry, she’s hungry. If I sign up to join a club, so will she. I mean, yeah, it’s kind of weird, but I get it. She just wants a friend. But, if I don’t get some privacy right now, I can’t be the friend that she needs in her life.

annotations.comment: See, here, you summarized very nicely and it works well.
Does the narrator have other friends at school?

paragraphs.body: The last couple of days have been stressful with Madeline being my shadow, and what I need most is just to be alone with my thoughts. I put the hood of my jacket over my hair and slump in my desk. The clock in the room is ticking loudly, making sure that I am aware of every grueling second that goes by as I wait for my personal space to be invaded by my roommate. My foot taps nervously over and over again on the tiled floor as I wait for class to begin. “Is this seat taken?” I hear from beside me.

I’m about to scream at her to get lost until I see who the voice belongs to. A girl. Her attractiveness is undeniable. Her long, golden hair is curled to perfection with wispy bangs blown out to shape her face. Her blue eyes are striking, a crystalline blue color, shimmering underneath the fluorescent lights of the classroom. Long lashes frame those beautiful eyes, adding to the intensity of her blue irises. Her skin is fair yet radiant, a hint of sunburn marking her shoulders and the tip of her nose. I can only imagine her beautiful figure stretched out on a beach over the summer, delicately sprawled out as the sun illuminates her. Her soft, full lips curve up into a smile as I continue to drink in the sight of her. I’ve had crushes before, but, shit, this feels surreal. “Nevermind, I can sit somewhere else,” she says, her warm, buttery voice dropping into something resembling disappointment.

“What? Oh, no, please go ahead. I’m sorry I was just zoning out,” I spit out hurriedly in an attempt to keep her around. I watch as she smiles and nods, taking a seat next to me. I catch a whiff of her subtle perfume, the sweet scent of lavender and vanilla entering my nostrils.

annotations.comment: Interested to see where this goes.

paragraphs.body: “Okay,” she says, her eyes squinting as she smiles.
“I’m Jesse.” I smile back at her.
“Claire,” she states, putting her perfectly manicured hand out. I her hand it in my own and shake it.

Claire is a breath of fresh air. She's beautiful, that's undeniable, but she’s funny, too, in a way that surprises me. Her hot breath from whispering to me all class still lingers in my ear, and the sound of her muffled giggles, hidden from the professor, feels addictive. It’s amazing how much you can learn about someone in seventy-five minutes: Claire’s a freshman, lives nearby, and her mom’s a professor here on campus. Her dad’s a vet, and her eyes light up talking about wanting to follow in his footsteps. She has two brothers, a sister, a pair of cats, and two dogs. And best of all? She’s totally into me.

annotations.comment: Now, this is a scene I would like to see, at least a little.

paragraphs.body: I stare at her number in my phone, grinning ear to ear as I unlock the door to my dorm and push it open. There’s Madeline, perched on her elevated twin XL, upright on her bright red comforter. She scrambles for a book and holds it up to her face a little too quickly.

annotations.comment: I wondered what she was doing?
annotations.context_left: it up to her face a

paragraphs.body: “Oh, hey, Mads,” I say, stepping inside.
"Hey! Did you have another class? I thought you were only taking four this semester?" she asks, putting the book down beside her and turning her full attention to me.
"I was on the waitlist for this one. Someone must’ve dropped, so I finally got in," I tell her, tossing my backpack aside and hopping onto my bed across the room.
"What’s the class called?" she asks, her gaze intense, her tone tight, like she's trying to keep her voice casual but not quite pulling it off.

annotations.comment: Watch that you don’t let the authorial voice creep in here too much — I buy that Mads’ voice sounds off. I don’t necessarily believe that she’s able to parse all of this out.

paragraphs.body: I pause, sensing something off but unsure what. "You okay?" I ask, letting a little concern slip into my voice.

annotations.comment: Yes, this makes better sense.

paragraphs.body: She forces a bright smile. "Yeah, of course! Why wouldn’t I be? Just tired after today, I guess. So, what class did you get into?"
“It’s called ‘Social Deviance,’” I tell her absently, eyes drifting down to my phone as Claire’s texts come through.

annotations.comment: Oh, love this moment.

paragraphs.body: Claire: Hey, it’s Claire <3
Claire: Wanna grab dinner with me tonight?
me: You know it ;)
Claire: Meet me in the quad in 20 minutes :)

I throw my phone down with a grin, looking over at Madeline, but her smile’s gone. She’s looking out the window, her face turned away, shoulders tight. I’m about to ask her again if everything’s alright, but I stop myself.I haven’t seen her like this before. She’s usually so full of energy and never wants to stop talking. The last few nights all we’ve done is stay up and talk for hours about nonsense at Gary’s, but this is a whole new Madeline. My phone buzzes with a new message from Claire:

annotations.comment: I wondered a little about the intensity here. It’s hard to figure out if Jesse is still thinking everything is great with Maddy — if so, why not invite her along with Claire? Or, is she definitely starting to think things are weird — if so, why hang out with her four nights in a row?

paragraphs.body: Claire: Leaving my dorm now! I’ll see you in a bit :)

I bite my lip, looking at the message once more before turning my attention back up to Madeline who continues to stare out the window. I know I promised her that I’ll always be here for her, but she’ll be fine for a few hours, right? I go over to the mirror and fix my hair, brushing through my tangled brown locks before spritzing myself with a bit of cologne. I grab my staple leather jacket and stuff my wallet into one of its pockets. I take a deep breath and turn to Madeline. I hope she won't be mad at me. “Hey, I’m gonna head out for a bit,” I tell her. “Dinner plans.”
She nods, her voice neutral. “Have fun,” she says, never turning back to look at me.
I nod slowly before hurrying out the door to meet Claire in the quad.

annotations.comment: I wondered if perhaps Maddy might start acting like this when she learns Jesse has dinner plans? Or would somehow become even more difficult once that’s clear?
annotations.context_right: g out the door to meet Claire in the quad.

paragraphs.body: Ten minutes later, when I finally get to the quad, Claire is waiting for me. Her soft skin is radiant underneath the summer sun. She saunters towards me, waving and smiling. I wave back, smiling softly as the two of us stand face to face.
“You’re late,” she teases.
“Only by two minutes!” I try to defend myself. “I was having roommate troubles.”
Claire’s expression softens. “Oh no. Is she okay?”
“Yeah, I think she just had a rough day. She didn’t really want to talk about it,” I say with a shrug.
“Okay, well if you need to reschedule I am always down for a rain check,” she says with a bright smile.
“Are you kidding? I wouldn’t miss this for the world!” I hold out my arm for her dramatically, waiting for her to take it. “Shall we? I know a pretty good place we can go.”
“We shall. Let's hurry too. I’m starving,” she says and giggles, taking my arm as we walk through campus.

I lead Claire into Gary’s Diner to the back booth where I normally sit. She chats about her day, her laugh ringing out, and I feel this warm sensation every time she nudges my arm or laughs at my dumb jokes. But every now and then, a strange feeling pricks my skin, like something’s off. I look around the diner for its other occupants. Besides Claire and I seated in the back corner, there’s an older gentleman that sits at the counter, a steaming mug of coffee next to a plate of slightly-burnt bacon and eggs in front of him. Every now and then, a singular waitress checks in on us and the man before returning to the kitchen in the back, or taking a cigarette break outside the diner. I can see everyone inside the diner that appears to be minding their own business, though I can’t shake the nagging feeling that someone else is here. Claire grabs my hand, breaking me out of my trance of staring at our waitress pouring the man at the counter more coffee.

annotations.comment: I would suggest that you might think about how Claire can be more than just, like, nice and normal.

paragraphs.body: “Hey, Jesse, are you still with me?” she asks, her smile never fading away from her face.

“Yeah, sorry, I guess I was just lost in thought for a moment there,” I say and chuckle, running my free hand through my hair. Then the doorbell jingles—a sharp, jarring sound that breaks through the tense air in the diner. I spin around, expecting to see someone entering or the man at the counter leaving. But the doorway stands empty, and when I look back, the man and the waitress remain at the counter unmoving.

annotations.comment: Well, I am super intrigued to know what that sense is! Elizabeth, you’re doing a nice job with this novel. I really am interested in the situation and would love to know what finally happens! My biggest note is to keep going, but I would also suggest (besides other little things I mentioned) that you think about who these people are besides “college student” and “perhaps psychotic college student.” Not just their backgrounds, but who they are and what they want to do. It was interesting, for example, that Jesse assume the class was going to be boring. That suggested all kind of things about her reason for being in college at all, and I wanted to know more. I wondered what Maddy is studying. I wondered why Claire was able to be so easy and light in making friends. And also — that character work is the fun part! So have fun with it. :) Strong work! Happy 30 pages to go!
